text: Ladies and gentlemen, please welcome the wonderful John Tutoros!
My story begins.
I am driving my silver station Volvo from Brooklyn to my mother's house in Rosedale, Queens.
On a hot mid-afternoon August day in 2003.
My mother is a widow.
My father has passed away from lung cancer 15 years before in 1988.
She has not resumed dating. She has sworn off men, and no one certain terms.
She has told me that I am never ever going to wash another pair of men's underwear again.
I am finished with the species on done.
She is somewhere around 80 years old, but I don't know for sure because she has never told me how old she is.
Matter of fact, on my birth certificate you can see that she has altered her age.
I am very close with my mother. We have a very tight relationship.
We've always had this bond, this complicity, this silent love between us.
I check in with her almost every evening to make sure she is okay and to alleviate her loneliness.
The only time when I was in a film was when I was editing a film.
It was around 1230, one clock, and I felt this strange feeling in my body is if somebody was tapping me and saying,
maybe you should check in on your mother.
I just stood up and told my editor, I have to call my mother.
I called her up and she was having a heart attack.
She said, I'm fine, I don't worry, and she hung up.
I called her back and I was able to get the ambulance and myself there in a nick of time.
As long as I can remember, I've always been my mother's protector against my father, my brother and the rest of the world.
She also mine. I grew up in a very volatile house.
My father was a World War II veteran. He suffered from post-traumatic stress syndrome, which wasn't really diagnosed in those days.
My older brother, who also had problems lived downstairs.
As my father got sick from cancer, he lost his booming voice.
His voice faded away. My brother's voice in the basement rose.
I could tell that this was not a good sign for things to come.
When we grow up, we all think we're going to get married.
We're going to have our own family, and we're going to leave the other family behind, or siblings, and our parents.
But it doesn't actually always occur that way.
It's very hard to break those ties from the first family that formed you.
Anyway, we get into a car, my mother, that day, and we're going to visit my brother, Ralph, who no longer lives with my mother.
He now resides at the Creedmore Mental Health State Hospital.
He's lived there for the last seven years.
It's the psychiatric state hospital that's the one that's located in Queens, or if you can turn bike.
He's not too happy about a living there, but that's where he's been there for the last seven years.
He's been diagnosed as paranoid, schizophrenic, bipolar, obsessive, compulsive, borderline personality, any diagnosis that's out there, he's got it.
Okay, this is, you know, how it's been.
So he's also, you know, been to therapy, he's had shock treatments, he's had every combination of anti-psychotic drugs and all the side effects that go with it.
The weight gain, the teeth loss, the tremors, the shaking, the stiffness, diabetes, and this is, you know, this is the situation that he's in.
He's stable, we're actually allowed to take him out on a pass, and he loves to go out on passes because we take him shopping and he gets something to eat because he doesn't like the food there.
And he also loves to go to the hair salon.
My brother Ralph's hair is very important to him.
He doesn't have many teeth, but his hair has to be done just so.
You know, he studied to be a hairdresser, he went to the beauty institute and he used to practice on me doing sort of five hour haircuts.
Anyway, he likes his hair just so. If it's not just so his emotional state plumets and then we have to deal with that.
So we're we're on the way to Creedmore and Creedmore was a place he lives in building 40 at 17th story building.
He's in the lock facility on the 11th floor and Creedmore when we were kids.
We used to pass on the parkway with my father driving and it was a place where the bookie man lived.
There was a place where all the crazy people lived and used to say, you know, you don't want to wind up and Creedmore.
I was what we said to each other and now it's my brother's home.
And this is actually the first time I've ever spoken about it in public.
So we we get there. You have to go through two sets of doors which then you're locked into.
And I walked to the elevator. I pressed the button because you found the 11th floor.
Look at the bank of lights and as we're waiting for the elevator to descend in the lobby with the security personnel.
The lights go out. We don't know what happened. We're all looking around and it's two years after 9.11.
Everyone's a little jury with thinking, hey, maybe this could be another attack or something.
And lots of most people don't have cell phones. I don't have a cell phone.
About 10 minutes later they say it's a blackout. It's the blackout of 2003 which affected I think 50 million people knocked out on tario and eight states here in America.
But we don't know this at the time. We know that all I know is that my brother has a pass and he wants to go out and he's looking forward to it.
So I asked the guys can they call upstairs and talk to the doctor and they do and they say I can I can go up there.
So I asked my mother they said listen you wait down here I go upstairs. I start walking up the 11th. They're very long flights. It's kind of dark.
And I'm thinking, you know what a roller coaster mental illness is not just for the patient but for everyone else involved.

It's a sentence that you're given and it's a life sentence and it's you know there's all the things that you have to go through the the the doctors the drugs the violent outbursts the destruction literally and emotionally the police coming to your house.
The shame that you live with. It just goes on and on it's not like those movies like a beautiful mind with someone reaches out and says,
you know let me all you need is love you know.
Love is a given but it's a war of attrition. It really is it's a long endless baseball season that never ever ends.
It goes on and on and really what it is it's this grind. You have to have unbelievable patience and this emotional fortitude to survive and love can die also and they can also be renewed.
But to to to survive that that's what wears you out and it it kills a lot of people and that's why you see so many people out on the street.
Because they become their families flee and I don't blame them and they become words of the state. So anyway that's what's going through my mind as I come up and knock on the door.

My brother's happy to see me. I talked to the doctor and then his friend this young thin black man Isaiah who draws his life every day. He storyboards his entire existence comes over and shows me his latest masterpiece.
And I said it's very nice Isaiah. As I'm trying to deal with my brother and the doctor and he whispered in my ear can I come to with you.
And I said Isaiah listen I love to love to take you but I you know it's a black out and I'm going to take Ralph okay, so the doctor says okay.

So we go down as stairs we have to go down slow because my brother can't see so well because he had an altercation with a very huge patient who was an ex-prisoner from Rikers Island and the guy savagely beat him and now he's blind in one eye.
So I have to take him down. So I come down with my brother and my mom who's getting older come outside and of course we've brought him cigarettes.
Now I'm worried about the time because it was 410 when the black out happened by now it's around 5 o'clock. But my brother is in no hurry. I'm worried about the light.
Anyway, so I give him a cigarette. He can't have one cigarette. He has to have one after another and another and he smokes him down to the very tiny bit end.
And when you give him the pack you have to open it just so everything according to his specifications otherwise he will take the cigarettes out and break them.

That's kind of how symbolic of my relationship with him much of the time. I buy him cigarettes. He breaks them. I buy him a CD player. He rips off the cover. I renovate my mother's house. He burns it down. We get into car.

He has to sit in the back seat over here so I can see him in the mirror because it's precarious. My mother sits here. She's never driven. She doesn't know that much about driving but I want to keep my eye on him. So I'm driving.
There are no stop lights. I have to make sure I keep my eye on my brother who sometimes can punch the window at a frustration.
And I have to figure out where am I going? It's a black out. What are we going to do? I know he's hungry.
So my mother says, you know, why don't you make a left turn and I'm in the far right lane. I say, Mom, I can't go over three lanes like that.
But she seems kind of oblivious. It's getting later. We see the diner that we normally go to. We pull in. There's no one in there. But it seems open. So we get out.
And the guy looks at us. We walk in. He's just a big Greek guy with a wall or a mustache. And I say, are you serving? And he says, black out.
He goes, it's a black out.
Okay, fine. I know it's a black out. My brother says, look, he says, I want to cheeseburger.
It goes, no cheeseburger. Black out. Coffee. So my mother tries to alleviate referee the situation. She says, when the stove is going to, you must have a gas stove. He says, pilot light electric.
Coffee. No cheeseburger. So my brother, of course, keeps asking for the cheeseburger. Then he says, what about French fries?
And the guy goes, no, no, no. So my mother and my brother look at him and credulously look at this guy. What a weakling. There's a black out and he falls like a cheap suit.
They're like, look at him. I'm done explaining, Mom, it's a black out. You know, they're worried.
Anyway, I'm looking at the clock. It's getting later. I get him in a car after another cigarette. We're driving. Everything is closed. It's like a ghost town because people worry.
When there's a black out, they remember 1977. The stores are closed. The restaurants are closed. Even the hassle on is closed, which is very upsetting throughout.
We just keep driving and driving. I'm going, okay, it's going to get darker. And we don't know where we're going.
Plus, I have my own family back in Brooklyn, my wife and two kids. So finally, I see a little piece of meat on the corner.
And I pull over and I said, wow, I jump. I look so open. I run in the end. The guy has a wood burning oven.
And he says, yes, I'm open. He's an Italian guy, of course. That's good.
So I get them to come out. We sit outside on the table and we order a brick oven pizza and warm soda.
And they come. It takes a long time. I'm looking at my mother and thinking, wow, she's getting older.
I'm looking at my brother thinking, what's going to happen after she's gone? Who's going to take care of them?
I'm the middle child and one of three boys. I'm the responsible one for good and for bad.
I think, well, I'm going to be alone with him one day and it's going to just be me and him.
And my brother looks at me and he's very perceptive when he's gone. He can spot a person's weakness with startling accuracy and speed like that.
And he looks at me and he says, you know, you get a lot of material from me. Don't you?
I go, yeah. Yeah.
He goes, where would you be without me?
Anyway, we have the pizza, of course, Ralph wants ice cream, but it's all melted by that.
So he has kind of a milkshake ice cream sandwich, which is happy and he will sit down.
I gently, the sun is now setting, something. We got to get back.
So I finally get to Minocar, goes through the same ritual. We drive slowly. There's no street lights. There's no stop lights. It's starting to get dark.
We finally make it back to this big, 17th story for building, ugly, big building with bars, building 40.
The creed was psychiatric, state hospital, and I pull in. He had some more cigarettes.
And then I come in and leave my mother in the car and then I kind of help him in.
The place is not lit and it's not air conditioned anymore.
And so, you know, I really feel torn in all these directions.
My mother, my brother, my family, and Brooklyn.
I go with my brother, help him up the 11 flights.
And, you know, it's always hard to say goodbye to him, but this day it's even stranger,
because here he is in this place, and he's in the dark.
You know, in a dark place. And so, like a Mahal, I tell him I'll see him, you know, soon.
Come down, get my mother and I drive on the parkway to her house and Rosedale, which is a long trip.
And before we get to her house, I know she's not going to want to come to my house, because she wants to be at her house.
I find a little drugstore that's open and they do have some batteries because I'm worried about her flashlights and everything.
And because I wanted to be able to see, and I'm afraid she's going to fall.
So I get her in the house, the flashlight's work, check the refrigerator, food is actually still cold.
We light a candle, we sit there, and we talk a little bit.
And you know, I'm thinking, well, this is how it was, you know, for thousands of years.
People didn't have a electric, you know, they had a little fire, they talked, you know, and people invented stories, they invented religion.
You know, they'd all kind of think, you know.
I mean, you know, the three major religions, you know, is it's the orphaned desert.
You know, they came out of the desert. So anyway, make sure she's okay, you know, and then she's at the door, and remember that,
with the little flashlight and saying, be careful, drive carefully.
And again, in my car, and I make my way back, you know, to Brooklyn.
And you know, I'm thinking in the car, thinking like, you know, we imagine that we live in the light.
We imagine we know what's going to happen.
We imagine we can foresee what's going to happen. We imagine we can control everything.

You know, when I'm going to do this and I'm going to do that, and the reality is, truthfully, that almost all of us are just stumbling along in the dark, searching, trying to reach some kind of home, you know, while we're juggling all these balls, hopefully keeping them afloat.
And finally, I make my way back to Brooklyn, and then I, you know, walk back to my house, and I open the door to the rest of my life.
